input: The global two-level resource block allocation for URLLC and eMBB, incorporating inter-gNodeB borrowing, OFDMA exclusivity, per-user resource limits, eMBB minimum rates, and URLLC delay bounds, constitutes a non-linear binary optimization problem that is NP-hard via reduction from the 0–1 knapsack problem.

The global two-level resource block allocation for URLLC and eMBB with inter-gNodeB borrowing, OFDMA exclusivity, per-user resource limits, eMBB minimum rates, and URLLC delay bounds constitutes a linear continuous optimization that is solvable in polynomial time without any reduction from 0–1 knapsack.

The global two-level resource block allocation for URLLC and eMBB—with inter-gNodeB borrowing, OFDMA exclusivity, per-user resource limits, eMBB minimum rates, and URLLC delay bounds—constitutes a _________(non-linear binary/convex continuous) optimization problem that is NP-hard via reduction from the 0–1 knapsack problem.

The global two-level resource block allocation for URLLC and eMBB with inter-gNodeB borrowing, OFDMA exclusivity, per-user resource limits, eMBB minimum rates, and URLLC delay bounds is NP-hard via reduction from _________(the 0–1 knapsack problem/maximum flow).

Assertion (A): The global two-level resource block allocation for URLLC and eMBB, with inter-gNodeB borrowing, OFDMA exclusivity, per-user resource limits, eMBB minimum rates, and URLLC delay bounds, forms a non-linear binary optimization that is NP-hard via reduction from the 0–1 knapsack problem. Reason (R): A reduction from the 0–1 knapsack problem demonstrates NP-hardness for a binary formulation with non-linear coupling induced by the listed constraints.
output: A is True, R is True, and R explains A

input: Assertion (A): The global two-level resource block allocation for URLLC and eMBB with inter-gNodeB borrowing, OFDMA exclusivity, per-user resource limits, eMBB minimum rates, and URLLC delay bounds constitutes a non-linear binary optimization that is NP-hard via reduction from the 0–1 knapsack problem. Reason (R): The problem is linear and continuous, so it is solvable in polynomial time without need for reductions.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which description matches a two time-scale SDN-based RAN slicing mechanism that dynamically adapts to traffic and channel variations while limiting controller–gNodeB signaling? [A] It allocates resource blocks to gNodeBs using a single-agent EXP3 bandit and performs per-user scheduling and cooperative inter-gNodeB resource sharing using multi-agent deep Q-learning. [B] It uses a single time-scale non-SDN design with centralized exhaustive search for resource block allocation and independent per-user scheduling at each gNodeB without cooperation, adapting only to average traffic while increasing signaling. [C] It applies a two time-scale SDN design with single-agent UCB1 for resource block allocation and multi-agent policy gradients for scheduling and cooperation, adapting to traffic but requiring frequent controller–gNodeB signaling. [D] It applies multi-agent deep Q-learning for resource block allocation and single-agent EXP3 for per-user scheduling, adapts solely to channel variations, and ignores signaling overhead.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Identify the mechanism that is SDN-based, operates on two time scales, allocates resource blocks to gNodeBs, performs per-user scheduling with cooperative inter-gNodeB resource sharing, adapts to traffic and channel variations, and limits controller–gNodeB signaling. [A] Uses Thompson sampling for resource block allocation and single-agent deep Q-learning without inter-gNodeB cooperation, operates at a constant time scale, and requires extensive signaling. [B] Uses heuristic round-robin for resource block allocation and centralized MILP for scheduling and sharing at the controller, yet claims to adapt and limit signaling. [C] Uses a single-agent EXP3 bandit for resource block allocation and multi-agent deep Q-learning for per-user scheduling with cooperative inter-gNodeB resource sharing, operating on two time scales and limiting signaling while adapting to traffic and channel variations. [D] Uses a single-agent UCB1 bandit for resource block allocation and multi-agent deep Q-learning without inter-gNodeB cooperation, operates on a single time scale, and prioritizes throughput regardless of signaling.

In a two time-scale SDN-based RAN slicing mechanism, resource blocks are allocated to gNodeBs using a single-agent EXP3 bandit, while per-user scheduling and cooperative inter-gNodeB resource sharing are performed using multi-agent deep Q-learning, enabling dynamic adaptation to traffic and channel variations and limiting controller–gNodeB signaling.

A single time-scale RAN slicing mechanism that allocates resource blocks via centralized exhaustive search and performs non-cooperative per-user scheduling at each gNodeB dynamically adapts to traffic and channel variations while limiting controller–gNodeB signaling in an SDN-based system.

A two time-scale SDN-based RAN slicing mechanism that allocates resource blocks to gNodeBs using a _________(single-agent EXP3 bandit/single-agent UCB1 bandit) and performs per-user scheduling and cooperative inter-gNodeB resource sharing using multi-agent deep Q-learning dynamically adapts to traffic and channel variations while limiting controller–gNodeB signaling.

A two time-scale SDN-based RAN slicing mechanism that allocates resource blocks to gNodeBs using a single-agent EXP3 bandit and performs per-user scheduling and cooperative inter-gNodeB resource sharing using _________(multi-agent deep Q-learning/single-agent tabular Q-learning) dynamically adapts to traffic and channel variations while limiting controller–gNodeB signaling.

Assertion (A): A two time-scale SDN-based RAN slicing mechanism that allocates resource blocks to gNodeBs using a single-agent EXP3 bandit and performs per-user scheduling and cooperative inter-gNodeB resource sharing via multi-agent deep Q-learning adapts dynamically to traffic and channel variations while limiting controller–gNodeB signaling. Reason (R): Separating decisions across two time scales and using EXP3 for resource block allocation with multi-agent deep Q-learning for scheduling and cooperation enables responsiveness to changing conditions without frequent controller–gNodeB exchanges.
output: A is True, R is True, and R explains A

input: Assertion (A): A two time-scale SDN-based RAN slicing mechanism that uses a single-agent EXP3 bandit for resource block allocation and multi-agent deep Q-learning for per-user scheduling and cooperative inter-gNodeB resource sharing adapts dynamically to traffic and channel variations while limiting controller–gNodeB signaling. Reason (R): All decisions are centralized at a single time scale in the controller, eliminating the need for learning at gNodeBs.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Packet delays for URLLC and eMBB traffic are modeled with an _________(M/M/1/G/G/1) queuing system, and minimum-rate and maximum-delay constraints are enforced within the allocation and learning processes.

Minimum-rate and maximum-delay constraints are enforced within the _________(allocation and learning/allocation only) processes while packet delays for URLLC and eMBB are modeled with an M/M/1 queuing system.

Assertion (A): Packet delays for URLLC and eMBB traffic are modeled with an M/M/1 queuing system, and minimum-rate and maximum-delay constraints are enforced within the allocation and learning processes. Reason (R): Minimum-rate and maximum-delay constraints are incorporated during both allocation and learning.
output: A is True, R is True, but R does not explain A

input: Assertion (A): Packet delays for URLLC and eMBB traffic are modeled with an M/M/1 queuing system, and minimum-rate and maximum-delay constraints are enforced within the allocation and learning processes. Reason (R): Delays are modeled with a G/G/1 queue and constraints are enforced only after allocation is completed.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): How is support for multiple 5G NR OFDM numerologies achieved in the described system? [A] By selecting numerology-specific trained models at the gNodeB based on channel state information [B] By deploying a single universal model at the UE regardless of channel conditions [C] By statically fixing one numerology per cell without using models or CSI [D] By random numerology hopping independent of model training or CSI

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which combination correctly describes the mechanism enabling support for multiple 5G NR OFDM numerologies? [A] Selection at the UE using a generic model based on QoS class [B] Selection at the gNodeB of numerology-specific trained models based on channel state information [C] Selection at the core network using heuristic tables based on subscription tier [D] No selection; a single standard numerology is applied uniformly
Support for multiple 5G NR OFDM numerologies is achieved by selecting numerology-specific trained models at the _________(gNodeB/UE) based on channel state information.
Support for multiple 5G NR OFDM numerologies is achieved by selecting numerology-specific trained models at the gNodeB based on _________(channel state information/traffic volume).

Assertion (A): Support for multiple 5G NR OFDM numerologies is achieved by selecting numerology-specific trained models at the gNodeB based on channel state information. Reason (R): Channel state information enables the gNodeB to choose the most suitable numerology-specific model for prevailing radio conditions.
output: A is True, R is True, and R explains A

input: Assertion (A): Support for multiple 5G NR OFDM numerologies is achieved by selecting numerology-specific trained models at the gNodeB based on channel state information. Reason (R): The selection is performed at the UE using a single model that ignores channel variations.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): To allow BOINC applications to execute on heterogeneous volunteer hosts without per-architecture porting and to provide sandbox isolation plus system-level checkpointing and recovery, which setup should be used? [A] Running the BOINC applications inside lightweight virtual machines that embed a BOINC client [B] Distributing host-native binaries compiled separately for each CPU architecture and running them directly on the host OS [C] Packaging the applications in OS-level containers that rely on the host kernel and an external BOINC client [D] Running the applications directly on volunteer hosts with user-level sandboxing and application-level checkpoint files

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement correctly characterizes running BOINC applications inside lightweight virtual machines that embed a BOINC client? [A] It supports heterogeneous hosts but still requires per-architecture porting and only offers application-level checkpointing without isolation [B] It restricts execution to homogeneous hosts while providing sandbox isolation and system-level checkpointing and recovery [C] It enables execution on heterogeneous volunteer hosts without per-architecture porting and provides sandbox isolation and system-level checkpointing and recovery [D] It eliminates the need for checkpointing by relying on host OS process management and disables isolation for performance

Running BOINC applications inside lightweight virtual machines that embed a BOINC client allows them to run on heterogeneous volunteer hosts without per-architecture porting and provides sandbox isolation plus system-level checkpointing and recovery.

Running BOINC applications inside lightweight virtual machines that embed a BOINC client requires per-architecture porting and offers no sandbox isolation or system-level checkpointing and recovery for heterogeneous volunteer hosts.

Assertion (A): Running BOINC applications inside lightweight virtual machines that embed a BOINC client enables execution on heterogeneous volunteer hosts without per-architecture porting, while providing sandbox isolation and system-level checkpointing and recovery. Reason (R): Virtual machines abstract hardware differences by encapsulating a uniform guest stack and support isolation and snapshot-based checkpointing.
output: A is True, R is True, and R explains A

input: Assertion (A): Running BOINC applications inside lightweight virtual machines that embed a BOINC client enables execution on heterogeneous volunteer hosts without per-architecture porting, while providing sandbox isolation and system-level checkpointing and recovery. Reason (R): Heterogeneous volunteer hosts already share a single hardware architecture and uniform OS environment, eliminating the need for isolation and checkpointing.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Mountable dependency disks carrying application software stacks allow BOINC applications with complex dependencies to run without modifying host environments and without inflating the base virtual machine image.

Running BOINC applications with complex dependencies necessarily requires either host environment modifications or enlarging the base virtual machine image, even when using mountable dependency disks carrying application software stacks.

Reason (R): Required software stacks are delivered on a separate, mountable disk rather than being installed in the host or baked into the base image.
output: A is True, R is True, and R explains A

input: Assertion (A): Mountable dependency disks carrying application software stacks enable BOINC applications with complex dependencies to run without modifying host environments or inflating the base virtual machine image. Reason (R): The base virtual machine image is intentionally enlarged to include all dependencies.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Using headless VirtualBox with bridged networking and guest control APIs on Linux and macOS provides unique guest addressing, enables remote management, supports VM snapshotting, and maintains user transparency.
On Linux and macOS, running headless VirtualBox with bridged networking and guest control APIs fails to deliver unique guest addressing and does not maintain user transparency.

Assertion (A): Using headless VirtualBox with bridged networking and guest control APIs satisfies the need for unique guest addressing, remote management, and VM snapshotting while maintaining user transparency on Linux and macOS. Reason (R): Bridged networking gives each VM a unique network identity, the guest control APIs enable remote operations, VirtualBox supports snapshots, and headless execution avoids visible UI on Linux and macOS.

input: Assertion (A): Using headless VirtualBox with bridged networking and guest control APIs satisfies the need for unique guest addressing, remote management, and VM snapshotting while maintaining user transparency on Linux and macOS. input: Compared to native BOINC, performance differences in V-BOINC are chiefly caused by virtualization-imposed resource constraints, and the V-BOINC control layer adds only negligible additional overhead.
Relative to native BOINC, performance differences are largely attributable to substantial overhead introduced by the V-BOINC control layer rather than by virtualization-imposed constraints.

In comparisons to native BOINC, the V-BOINC control layer introduces _________(negligible/substantial) additional overhead while performance differences are mainly driven by virtualization-imposed resource constraints.

Assertion (A): Relative to native BOINC, performance differences are primarily due to virtualization-imposed resource constraints, and the V-BOINC control layer introduces negligible additional overhead. Reason (R): Virtualization imposes resource limits, whereas the V-BOINC control layer adds minimal processing overhead.

input: Assertion (A): Relative to native BOINC, performance differences are primarily due to virtualization-imposed resource constraints, with negligible additional overhead from the V-BOINC control layer. Reason (R): The V-BOINC control layer performs intensive computation during task execution, which dominates performance costs.

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): For periodic virtual machine snapshots, which statement accurately describes both their effect on job progress during host interruptions and the relative storage/time overheads across workload types? [A] They preserve progress across host interruptions and typically have low storage and time overhead for CPU-bound workloads, but overhead increases for memory- or disk-intensive tasks. [B] They preserve progress only for memory- or disk-intensive tasks and have uniform overhead across workloads. [C] They fail to preserve progress across host interruptions and impose negligible overhead on all workloads. [D] They preserve progress across host interruptions but require uniformly high storage and time overhead regardless of workload type.

Choose the correct option (A–D): In a system using periodic virtual machine snapshots, what is true about preserving job progress across host interruptions and overhead levels for CPU-bound versus memory- or disk-intensive tasks? [A] They preserve job progress across host interruptions, with higher storage and time overheads for CPU-bound workloads than for memory- or disk-intensive tasks. [B] They do not affect job progress across host interruptions, but overheads are low for CPU-bound workloads and higher for memory- or disk-intensive tasks. [C] They preserve job progress across host interruptions and usually incur low storage and time overheads for CPU-bound workloads, with overheads increasing for memory- or disk-intensive tasks. [D] They preserve job progress only if snapshots are taken after interruptions, and overhead is independent of workload type.

With periodic virtual machine snapshots, storage and time overheads are typically _________(low/high) for CPU-bound workloads, while they increase for memory- or disk-intensive tasks, and job progress is preserved across host interruptions.

Periodic virtual machine snapshots _________(preserve/discard) job progress across host interruptions while usually incurring low storage and time overheads for CPU-bound workloads and higher overheads for memory- or disk-intensive tasks.

Assertion (A): Periodic virtual machine snapshots preserve job progress across host interruptions and typically impose low storage and time overheads for CPU-bound workloads, with higher overheads for memory- or disk-intensive tasks. Reason (R): Periodic snapshots create recoverable save points that maintain progress after interruptions, and workload type influences storage and time costs.
output: A is True, R is True, and R explains A

input: Assertion (A): Periodic virtual machine snapshots preserve job progress across host interruptions and usually incur low storage and time overheads for CPU-bound workloads, with overheads increasing for memory- or disk-intensive tasks. Reason (R): Overheads are identical across CPU-bound, memory-intensive, and disk-intensive workloads, and snapshots do not preserve progress after host interruptions.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): The sampling-invariant metric for comparing manifold topology across data partitions is provided by [A] a point-count-normalized sum of Wasserstein distances between persistence barcodes [B] an unnormalized average of Wasserstein distances between persistence barcodes [C] a point-count-normalized sum of bottleneck distances between persistence barcodes [D] a maximum of Wasserstein distances between persistence barcodes

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement correctly describes how to obtain a sampling-invariant metric for comparing manifold topology across data partitions? [A] Use a point-count-normalized sum of Wasserstein distances between persistence barcodes [B] Use an unnormalized sum of Wasserstein distances between persistence barcodes [C] Use a point-count-normalized sum of Wasserstein distances between raw point sets [D] Use a point-count-normalized median of bottleneck distances between persistence barcodes
A point-count-normalized sum of Wasserstein distances between persistence barcodes provides a sampling-invariant metric for comparing manifold topology across data partitions.
An unnormalized sum of Wasserstein distances between persistence barcodes provides a sampling-invariant metric for comparing manifold topology across data partitions.

A _________(point-count-normalized/variance-weighted) sum of Wasserstein distances between persistence barcodes provides a sampling-invariant metric for comparing manifold topology across data partitions.
A point-count-normalized sum of _________(Wasserstein/bottleneck) distances between persistence barcodes provides a sampling-invariant metric for comparing manifold topology across data partitions.

Assertion (A): A point-count-normalized sum of Wasserstein distances between persistence barcodes provides a sampling-invariant metric for comparing manifold topology across data partitions. Reason (R): Normalizing by the number of points per partition removes sample-size effects when summing Wasserstein distances.
output: A is True, R is True, and R explains A

input: Assertion (A): A point-count-normalized sum of Wasserstein distances between persistence barcodes provides a sampling-invariant metric for comparing manifold topology across data partitions. Reason (R): Wasserstein distance between persistence barcodes is inherently independent of sampling density even without normalization.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which option accurately matches the method, indicators, dataset scope, and outcomes? [A] A semi-supervised text mining approach using word-embedding–expanded ESG lexicons, valence-aware sentiment scoring, and rigorous filtering to build time-varying entity-level news indicators generates signals that, in a Dutch-language news corpus of European companies, anticipate ESG rating downgrades and enhance portfolio screening. [B] A fully supervised text mining approach using static ESG dictionaries without embeddings and polarity-agnostic scoring yields sector-level indicators that, in an English-language US news corpus, only track current ESG ratings without improving screening. [C] An unsupervised topic model with unfiltered sentiment produces company-level indicators that, in multilingual global news, forecast ESG rating upgrades but do not aid portfolio screening. [D] A rule-based system using manual ESG lexicons and neutral sentiment handling produces time-invariant firm indicators that, in French news on European firms, correlate weakly with subsequent downgrades and hinder screening.

[A] Supervised model with static lexicons; German-language news on European companies; anticipates ESG rating upgrades; marginal screening impact. [B] Semi-supervised model with word-embedding–expanded ESG lexicons, valence-aware sentiment, and rigorous filtering forming time-varying entity-level indicators; Dutch-language news on European companies; anticipates ESG rating downgrades and enhances portfolio screening. [C] Heuristic lexicon expansion without sentiment valence; English-language global news; detects only contemporaneous ESG levels; no screening benefit. [D] Weakly supervised classifier with minimal filtering; Spanish corporate news; anticipates credit rating downgrades; degrades screening.

A semi-supervised text mining approach using word-embedding–expanded ESG lexicons, valence-aware sentiment scoring, and rigorous filtering to build time-varying entity-level news indicators yields signals that, in a Dutch-language news corpus of European companies, anticipate ESG rating downgrades and enhance portfolio screening.

A semi-supervised text mining approach using word-embedding–expanded ESG lexicons, valence-aware sentiment scoring, and rigorous filtering to build time-varying entity-level news indicators yields signals that, in an English-language news corpus of US companies, anticipate ESG rating upgrades and do not enhance portfolio screening.

A semi-supervised text mining approach using word-embedding–expanded ESG lexicons, valence-aware sentiment scoring, and rigorous filtering to build time-varying entity-level news indicators produces signals that, in a _________(Dutch-language/German-language) news corpus of European companies, anticipate ESG rating downgrades and enhance portfolio screening.

A _________(semi-supervised/unsupervised) text mining approach that uses word-embedding–expanded ESG lexicons, valence-aware sentiment scoring, and rigorous filtering to build time-varying entity-level news indicators produces signals that, in a Dutch-language news corpus of European companies, anticipate ESG rating downgrades and enhance portfolio screening.

Assertion (A): A semi-supervised text mining approach using word-embedding–expanded ESG lexicons, valence-aware sentiment scoring, and rigorous filtering to form time-varying entity-level news indicators produces signals that, in Dutch-language news on European companies, anticipate ESG rating downgrades and enhance portfolio screening. Reason (R): Expanding ESG lexicons with word embeddings, incorporating valence-aware sentiment, and applying rigorous filtering produce cleaner, informative entity-level time series from Dutch news that surface early negative ESG cues useful for screening.
output: A is True, R is True, and R explains A

input: Assertion (A): A semi-supervised text mining approach using word-embedding–expanded ESG lexicons, valence-aware sentiment scoring, and rigorous filtering to build time-varying entity-level news indicators generates signals that, in Dutch-language news about European companies, anticipate ESG rating downgrades and improve portfolio screening. Reason (R): The effect occurs because the corpus is English-language news about US companies.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which option fully and correctly characterizes an objective that maximizes the expected integrand under the worst-case distribution within a χ2-divergence ball around the empirical context distribution? [A] It defines a distributionally robust Bayesian quadrature objective that reduces to a variance-regularized risk in a high-variance regime, admits efficient inner minimization via KKT-based bisection, and can be optimized by posterior sampling with sublinear Bayesian regret under standard kernel assumptions. [B] It defines a classical Bayesian quadrature objective that reduces to an unregularized risk in a low-variance regime, requires gradient descent for the inner minimization, and is optimized by UCB with linear Bayesian regret under standard kernel assumptions. [C] It defines a distributionally robust objective using a KL-divergence ball that reduces to a mean-regularized risk in a high-variance regime, requires Newton's method for the inner minimization, and has no regret guarantees for posterior sampling. [D] It defines a Wasserstein-robust Bayesian quadrature objective that reduces to a variance-regularized risk in a low-variance regime, admits closed-form inner minimization, and achieves sublinear regret only with greedy selection.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Identify the combination that jointly specifies the divergence set, the regime reduction, the inner minimization technique, and the optimization method with its regret guarantee for an objective that maximizes the expected integrand under a worst-case distribution around the empirical context distribution. [A] KL-divergence ball; low-variance → mean-regularized risk; projected gradient descent; UCB with linear regret. [B] Wasserstein ball; high-variance → variance-regularized risk; closed-form minimization; greedy selection without regret guarantees. [C] χ2-divergence ball; high-variance → variance-regularized risk; KKT-based bisection; posterior sampling with sublinear Bayesian regret under standard kernel assumptions. [D] Total variation ball; regime-independent; interior-point method; Thompson sampling with superlinear regret.

Maximizing the expected integrand under the worst-case distribution within a _________(χ2-divergence/KL-divergence) ball around the empirical context distribution defines a distributionally robust Bayesian quadrature objective that reduces to a variance-regularized risk in a high-variance regime, admits efficient inner minimization via KKT-based bisection, and can be optimized by posterior sampling with sublinear Bayesian regret under standard kernel assumptions.

Maximizing the expected integrand under the worst-case distribution within a χ2-divergence ball around the empirical context distribution defines a distributionally robust Bayesian quadrature objective that reduces to a variance-regularized risk in a high-variance regime, admits efficient inner minimization via _________(KKT-based bisection/Newton's method), and can be optimized by posterior sampling with sublinear Bayesian regret under standard kernel assumptions.

Assertion (A): Maximizing the expected integrand under the worst-case distribution within a χ2-divergence ball around the empirical context distribution defines a distributionally robust Bayesian quadrature objective that reduces to a variance-regularized risk in a high-variance regime, admits efficient inner minimization via KKT-based bisection, and can be optimized by posterior sampling with sublinear Bayesian regret under standard kernel assumptions. Reason (R): The χ2-divergence constraint yields a dual problem characterized by KKT conditions suitable for bisection, high variance makes variance penalization dominate the risk, and standard kernel posterior concentration enables sublinear-regret posterior sampling.
output: A is True, R is True, and R explains A

input: Assertion (A): Maximizing the expected integrand under the worst-case distribution within a χ2-divergence ball around the empirical context distribution defines a distributionally robust Bayesian quadrature objective that reduces to a variance-regularized risk in a high-variance regime, admits efficient inner minimization via KKT-based bisection, and can be optimized by posterior sampling with sublinear Bayesian regret under standard kernel assumptions. Reason (R): The inner worst-case problem is nonconvex and lacks KKT structure, so only heuristic search is feasible and posterior sampling cannot provide any regret guarantees.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): When boundary-point features are contrasted with local same-class positives and different-class negatives, and multi-scale sub-scene boundary mining is applied to sub-sampled point clouds, what is the reported effect across diverse architectures and datasets? [A] Enhanced feature discrimination across scene boundaries with consistent improvements in boundary alignment and overall point cloud segmentation performance [B] Improved global context modeling with no change in boundary alignment and decreased overall segmentation performance [C] Only boundary alignment improves in one architecture, with no effect on overall segmentation [D] Inconsistent gains limited to training data and degraded boundary discrimination

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which combination of strategy and outcome correctly characterizes the described approach? [A] Contrasting boundary-point features with local same-class positives and different-class negatives, plus multi-scale sub-scene boundary mining on sub-sampled point clouds, yields enhanced cross-boundary feature discrimination and consistent improvements in boundary alignment and overall segmentation across diverse architectures and datasets [B] Contrasting boundary-point features only with global negatives, paired with single-scale whole-scene mining, yields limited gains confined to a specific dataset and no consistent boundary alignment benefits [C] Contrasting random points irrespective of class labels, without boundary mining, yields improvements restricted to non-boundary regions on a single architecture [D] Contrasting boundary points with distant same-class positives and performing global scene aggregation yields better feature discrimination but no improvement in overall segmentation or boundary alignment

Contrasting boundary-point features with local same-class positives and different-class negatives and applying multi-scale sub-scene boundary mining to sub-sampled point clouds enhances feature discrimination across scene boundaries and consistently improves boundary alignment and overall point cloud segmentation performance across diverse architectures and datasets.

A configuration that contrasts boundary-point features with non-local positives while ignoring different-class negatives and omits multi-scale sub-scene boundary mining to sub-sampled point clouds still enhances cross-boundary feature discrimination and consistently improves boundary alignment and overall segmentation across diverse architectures and datasets.

_________(Contrastive boundary learning/Multi-head self-attention) that contrasts boundary-point features with local same-class positives and different-class negatives and applies multi-scale sub-scene boundary mining to sub-sampled point clouds enhances feature discrimination across scene boundaries and consistently improves boundary alignment and overall point cloud segmentation performance across diverse architectures and datasets.

It applies _________(multi-scale sub-scene boundary mining/single-scale global scene aggregation) to sub-sampled point clouds while contrasting boundary-point features with local same-class positives and different-class negatives, enhancing feature discrimination across scene boundaries and consistently improving boundary alignment and overall point cloud segmentation across diverse architectures and datasets.

Assertion (A): Contrasting boundary-point features with local same-class positives and different-class negatives and applying multi-scale sub-scene boundary mining to sub-sampled point clouds enhances feature discrimination across scene boundaries and consistently improves boundary alignment and overall point cloud segmentation performance across diverse architectures and datasets. Reason (R): These boundary-focused contrastive pairings and multi-scale mining provide boundary-aware supervision that generalizes across architectures and datasets.
output: A is True, R is True, and R explains A

input: Assertion (A): Contrasting boundary-point features with local same-class positives and different-class negatives and applying multi-scale sub-scene boundary mining to sub-sampled point clouds enhances feature discrimination across scene boundaries and consistently improves boundary alignment and overall point cloud segmentation performance across diverse architectures and datasets. Reason (R): The improvements are confined to a single network architecture and do not transfer across datasets.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement accurately characterizes personalized federated learning and what governs its balance between local and global learning? [A] Personalized federated learning is captured by a two-level Bayesian hierarchical formulation in which inter-client and intra-client uncertainties determine posterior-optimal personal and global targets, thereby governing the trade-off between local adaptation and global sharing. [B] Personalized federated learning uses a single-level frequentist model where deterministic aggregation weights define a fixed balance between local and global learning independent of uncertainty. [C] Personalized federated learning is modeled as a non-Bayesian multi-task framework in which only inter-client variability selects global targets while local adaptation is predetermined. [D] Personalized federated learning relies on heuristic personalization rules and treats uncertainties as irrelevant to choosing personal and global objectives.

Choose the correct option (A–D): How is personalized federated learning formulated, and what determines the personal and global targets and the resulting trade-off? [A] It is captured by a two-level Bayesian hierarchical model where only intra-client uncertainty sets personal targets and global targets are fixed, leaving the local–global trade-off unaffected by uncertainty. [B] It is captured by a two-level Bayesian hierarchical model in which inter-client and intra-client uncertainties determine posterior-optimal personal and global targets and thus the trade-off between local adaptation and global sharing. [C] It is described by a flat Bayesian model where posterior-optimal targets are global only and the local–global trade-off is imposed externally. [D] It is expressed as a two-level frequentist hierarchy where prior variances calibrate the trade-off without producing posterior-optimal targets.

Personalized federated learning is captured by a two-level Bayesian hierarchical formulation in which inter-client and intra-client uncertainties determine posterior-optimal personal and global targets and thus the trade-off between local adaptation and global sharing.

Personalized federated learning is adequately modeled by a single-level framework in which uncertainties across and within clients do not influence personal or global targets or the balance between local adaptation and global sharing.

Personalized federated learning is captured by a _________(two-level Bayesian hierarchical/single-level frequentist) formulation in which inter-client and intra-client uncertainties determine posterior-optimal personal and global targets and thus the trade-off between local adaptation and global sharing.

In this view, inter-client and intra-client uncertainties determine posterior-optimal personal and global targets and thereby the _________(trade-off between local adaptation and global sharing/benefit of centralized training) in personalized federated learning modeled as a two-level Bayesian hierarchical formulation.
output: trade-off between local adaptation and global sharing

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): In federated deep learning across rounds, how can inter- and intra-client uncertainties be handled, what does this enable, and what is the overhead relative to standard FedAvg? input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement correctly characterizes how an uncertainty-based formulation behaves under varying client conditions and its effect on personalization? [A] It recovers standard federated averaging when inter-client variation is negligible and reduces to local-only learning when clients are weakly connected or one client's data quality dominates, thereby self-tuning the degree of personalization [B] It reduces to local-only learning when inter-client variation is negligible and recovers standard federated averaging when clients are weakly connected, thereby fixing the degree of personalization [C] It always performs federated averaging regardless of variation or connectivity, thereby eliminating personalization [D] It always performs local-only learning regardless of variation or connectivity, thereby maximizing personalization

[A] Global averaging when inter-client variation is high; local-only when connectivity is strong; personalization is manually set [B] Global averaging when one client's data quality dominates; local-only when inter-client variation is negligible; personalization is fixed [C] Global averaging when inter-client variation is negligible; local-only when clients are weakly connected or one client's data quality dominates; personalization is self-tuned [D] It switches between global and local learning irrespective of conditions, leading to unstable personalization

When inter-client variation is negligible, an uncertainty-based formulation recovers _________(standard federated averaging/local-only learning), and when clients are weakly connected or one client's data quality dominates, it reduces to local-only learning, thereby self-tuning the degree of personalization.

Assertion (A): An uncertainty-based formulation recovers standard federated averaging when inter-client variation is negligible and reduces to local-only learning when clients are weakly connected or one client's data quality dominates, thereby self-tuning the degree of personalization. Reason (R): The uncertainty mechanism downweights cross-client aggregation under weak connectivity or skewed data quality and upweights it when clients are similar, yielding appropriate personalization.

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): In CBRS-style 5G spectrum sharing, which statement best describes the adversary’s capabilities and comparative jamming properties when training a surrogate classifier from local sensing and ACK observations to predict successful transmissions? [A] The adversary, without direct access to the target model’s inputs or outputs, can use local sensing and ACK observations to train a surrogate that predicts successful transmissions and then selectively jam either the sensing phase to force misdetections or the data phase to cause failures, with sensing-phase jamming being stealthier and more energy-efficient. [B] The adversary requires direct access to the target model’s inputs and outputs and can only jam the data phase, which is stealthier and more energy-efficient than jamming during sensing. [C] The adversary can train a surrogate only from control-plane logs and channel state information, and jamming the sensing phase is less stealthy and less energy-efficient than data-phase jamming. [D] The adversary cannot predict successful transmissions and can only indiscriminately jam both phases, with no difference in stealth or energy efficiency between phases.

Choose the correct option (A–D): In CBRS-style 5G spectrum sharing, how can an adversary exploit local sensing and ACK observations, and what is the relative stealth and energy profile of jamming choices, given no direct access to the target model’s inputs or outputs? [A] Use them to reconstruct the target model exactly and jam only after transmissions, since post-data jamming is the most stealthy and energy-saving. [B] Use them to train a surrogate that predicts successful transmissions and selectively jam the sensing phase to force misdetections or the data phase to cause failures, with sensing-phase jamming being stealthier and more energy-efficient. [C] They are insufficient for any predictive capability, making selective jamming impossible and offering no stealth advantage to either phase. [D] They enable predicting only failed transmissions, and sensing-phase jamming is louder and more power-hungry than data-phase jamming.

In CBRS-style 5G spectrum sharing, an adversary can train a surrogate classifier from local sensing and ACK observations to predict successful transmissions and selectively jam the sensing phase to force misdetections or the data phase to cause failures, with _________(sensing-phase jamming/data-phase jamming) being stealthier and more energy-efficient despite no direct access to the target model’s inputs or outputs.

In CBRS-style 5G spectrum sharing, an adversary can use local sensing and ACK observations to train a surrogate that predicts successful transmissions and choose to jam the sensing phase to cause misdetections or the data phase to cause failures, while operating _________(without direct access/with direct access) to the target model’s inputs or outputs, and sensing-phase jamming remains more stealthy and energy-efficient.

Assertion (A): In CBRS-style 5G spectrum sharing, an adversary lacking direct access to the target model’s inputs or outputs can train a surrogate from local sensing and ACK observations to predict successful transmissions and selectively jam either the sensing phase to cause misdetections or the data phase to cause failures, with sensing-phase jamming being more stealthy and energy-efficient. Reason (R): ACK feedback correlates with successful transmissions and early interference during sensing can induce low-energy misdetections that are harder to detect, enabling surrogate-guided selective jamming.
output: A is True, R is True, and R explains A

input: Assertion (A): In CBRS-style 5G spectrum sharing, surrogate training from local sensing and ACK observations enables an adversary to predict successful transmissions and selectively jam either sensing or data phases, with sensing-phase jamming being stealthier and more energy-efficient, even without direct access to the target model’s inputs or outputs. Reason (R): Direct access to the target model’s inputs and outputs is necessary to train any effective surrogate for selective jamming.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement accurately reflects that a generative adversarial network with distributed generator and discriminator nodes produces RF waveforms that imitate a legitimate 5G user's signals and consequently bypass deep-learning-based physical-layer authentication used for network slicing? Assertion (A): A generative adversarial network with distributed generator and discriminator nodes can synthesize RF waveforms that mimic a legitimate 5G user's signals and thereby evade deep-learning-based physical-layer authentication used for network slicing. [A] It reduces attacker surrogate-model uncertainty and improves performance with no impact on evasion or poisoning [B] It has negligible effect on attacker uncertainty and fails to mitigate evasion and poisoning while degrading performance [C] It increases attacker surrogate-model uncertainty, mitigates subsequent evasion and poisoning attacks, and limits performance degradation [D] It increases attacker uncertainty but substantially worsens performance

In 5G control, flipping a small, carefully selected subset of high-confidence machine-learning decisions lowers an attacker’s surrogate-model uncertainty, fails to mitigate evasion and poisoning attacks, and causes substantial performance degradation.

Assertion (A): Intentionally flipping a small, carefully selected subset of high-confidence machine-learning decisions in 5G control increases an attacker’s surrogate-model uncertainty and mitigates subsequent evasion and poisoning attacks while limiting performance degradation. input: Assertion (A): Deliberately flipping a small, carefully chosen subset of high-confidence decisions in 5G control raises attacker surrogate-model uncertainty, mitigates evasion and poisoning, and keeps performance degradation limited. input: Choose the correct option (A–D): For coordinated checkpoint I/O in HPC when node congestion is negligible, which comprehensive description correctly states the pattern regularity, zone partitioning and size trends with parallelism, storage-time dependence, and the feasibility of regression-based estimation of checkpoint duration and overhead-constrained checkpoint frequency? [A] It exhibits irregular spatial and temporal patterns partitioned into application data, library, and shared-memory zones whose sizes respectively shrink with increased parallelism, remain approximately constant per application, and grow with processes per node; storage time depends on zone size and access patterns; and regression can estimate checkpoint duration and overhead-constrained checkpoint frequency. [B] It exhibits regular patterns; zones are limited to application data and metadata; application data size grows with parallelism while shared-memory remains constant; storage time is mostly fixed and regression is unnecessary. [C] It exhibits irregular patterns partitioned into application data, library, and shared-memory zones; application data grows with parallelism, shared-memory remains constant, and library shrinks; storage time depends only on volume, and regression cannot estimate checkpoint frequency. [D] It exhibits irregular patterns partitioned into application data, library, and shared-memory zones with correct size trends, but storage time is independent of access patterns and regression is applicable only under severe congestion.

Choose the correct option (A–D): In the context of coordinated checkpoint I/O in HPC, identify the correct combination of pattern characteristics, zone size scaling with parallelism and processes per node, storage-time dependence, and the conditions under which regression-based models can estimate both checkpoint duration and overhead-constrained checkpoint frequency. [A] Regular patterns; zones are application data, library, and shared-memory; application data shrinks with parallelism, library is constant per application, shared-memory grows with processes per node; storage time depends on zone size and access patterns; regression works when node congestion is negligible. [B] Irregular patterns; zones are application data, library, and shared-memory; application data shrinks with parallelism, library is approximately constant per application, shared-memory grows with processes per node; storage time depends on zone size and access patterns; regression works when node congestion is negligible. [C] Irregular patterns; no meaningful zone partitioning; all zones remain constant with parallelism; storage time depends only on network bandwidth; regression works regardless of congestion. [D] Irregular patterns; zones are application data, library, and shared-memory; application data shrinks with parallelism, library is constant, shared-memory grows; storage time depends solely on zone sizes; regression can estimate only checkpoint duration, not frequency.

In coordinated checkpoint I/O in HPC, spatial and temporal access patterns are irregular and partition into application data, library, and shared-memory zones whose sizes respectively shrink with increased parallelism, remain approximately constant per application, and grow with processes per node, making storage time a function of zone size and access patterns and enabling regression-based estimation of checkpoint duration and overhead-constrained checkpoint frequency when node congestion is negligible.

In coordinated checkpoint I/O in HPC, patterns are regular and zones are limited to application data and library I/O, with all zone sizes growing with parallelism, making storage time independent of access patterns and preventing regression-based estimation of checkpoint duration and overhead-constrained checkpoint frequency even when node congestion is negligible.

Assertion (A): In coordinated checkpoint I/O in HPC, irregular spatial and temporal patterns partition into application data, library, and shared-memory zones whose sizes respectively shrink with increased parallelism, remain approximately constant per application, and grow with processes per node, making storage time a function of zone size and access patterns and permitting regression-based estimation of checkpoint duration and overhead-constrained checkpoint frequency when node congestion is negligible. Reason (R): When interference from other nodes is minimal, zone-specific scaling (application data shrinking with parallelism, library I/O remaining approximately constant per application, and shared-memory I/O growing with processes per node) means zone sizes and access patterns capture the main determinants of storage time, which regression can model.
output: A is True, R is True, and R explains A

input: Assertion (A): Coordinated checkpoint I/O in HPC exhibits irregular spatial and temporal patterns split into application data, library, and shared-memory zones with sizes that respectively shrink with increased parallelism, remain approximately constant per application, and grow with processes per node, enabling regression-based estimation of checkpoint duration and overhead-constrained checkpoint frequency when node congestion is negligible because storage time depends on zone size and access patterns. Reason (R): Node congestion, not zone sizes or access patterns, dominates storage time in these settings, making regression modeling ineffective.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement accurately describes a deep learning detector for passive RIS-assisted links in terms of architecture, inference target, input requirements, computational complexity, and robustness? [A] It uses fully connected layers with tanh activations to jointly infer channel fading and RIS phase shifts directly from received signals without pilot signaling, enabling symbol detection in passive RIS-assisted links with lower inference complexity than maximum-likelihood and MMSE detectors and robust error performance under imperfect CSI and mismatches in channel models or RIS element counts. [B] It uses convolutional layers with ReLU activations and requires pilot signaling to estimate channels, offers similar or higher inference complexity than maximum-likelihood and MMSE detectors, and is not robust to imperfect CSI or mismatches in channel models or RIS element counts. [C] It uses fully connected layers with tanh activations but infers only symbols from pilots, applies to active RIS-assisted links, and achieves lower complexity only under perfect CSI without model or element-count mismatches. [D] It uses recurrent layers with sigmoid activations to estimate RIS phases from received signals while relying on pilots for channel fading, achieving higher inference complexity than MMSE and fragile error performance under imperfect CSI or model/element-count mismatches.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which option best matches the full claim about a detector's architecture, inference approach, pilot usage, application setting, complexity, and robustness? [A] A convolutional ReLU-based network estimates symbols using pilot signaling in active RIS-assisted links and has higher inference complexity than maximum-likelihood and MMSE, with robustness only under perfect CSI. [B] A fully connected tanh network requires pilots to estimate the channel, focuses on symbol detection without inferring RIS phases, and matches the inference complexity and error performance of MMSE. [C] A fully connected deep network with tanh activations jointly infers channel fading and RIS phase shifts directly from received signals without pilot signaling, enabling symbol detection in passive RIS-assisted links with lower inference complexity than maximum-likelihood and MMSE detectors and robust error performance under imperfect CSI and under mismatches in channel models or RIS element counts. [D] A recurrent network jointly infers fading and RIS phases from pilots, applies to non-RIS links, and achieves lower complexity than MMSE only when CSI is perfect and model/RIS-count mismatches are absent.

A deep learning detector with fully connected layers and tanh activations can jointly infer channel fading and RIS phase shifts directly from received signals without pilot signaling, enabling symbol detection in passive RIS-assisted links with lower inference complexity than maximum-likelihood and MMSE detectors and robust error performance under imperfect CSI and under mismatches in channel models or RIS element counts.

A deep learning detector with fully connected layers and tanh activations cannot jointly infer channel fading and RIS phase shifts from received signals and therefore requires pilot signaling, resulting in higher inference complexity than maximum-likelihood and MMSE detectors and non-robust error performance under imperfect CSI or mismatches in channel models or RIS element counts.

A deep learning detector with fully connected layers and tanh activations jointly infers channel fading and RIS phase shifts directly from received signals without pilot signaling, enabling symbol detection in passive RIS-assisted links with _________(lower/higher) inference complexity than maximum-likelihood and MMSE detectors and robust error performance under imperfect CSI and mismatches in channel models or RIS element counts.

Assertion (A): A deep learning detector with fully connected layers and tanh activations jointly infers channel fading and RIS phase shifts directly from received signals without pilot signaling, enabling symbol detection in passive RIS-assisted links with lower inference complexity than maximum-likelihood and MMSE detectors and robust error performance under imperfect CSI and mismatches in channel models or RIS element counts. Reason (R): It infers channel fading and RIS phase shifts directly from received signals without pilot signaling.
output: A is True, R is True, but R does not explain A

input: Assertion (A): A deep learning detector with fully connected layers and tanh activations jointly infers channel fading and RIS phase shifts directly from received signals without pilot signaling, enabling symbol detection in passive RIS-assisted links with lower inference complexity than maximum-likelihood and MMSE detectors and robust error performance under imperfect CSI and mismatches in channel models or RIS element counts. Reason (R): It requires pilot signaling to estimate the channel and RIS phases before symbol detection.

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): For user-level depression detection from tweet histories, which description matches a framework that condenses each user's tweets via hybrid extractive–abstractive summarization and achieves the reported comparative performance? [A] It uses BERT embeddings with k-means selection followed by BART generation to summarize tweets, then late-fuses CNN–BiGRU attention-based text representations with stacked BiGRU behavioral features, yielding better classification than using raw posts or behavioral signals alone. [B] It skips summarization, relies solely on behavioral features with a single GRU, and performs no better than text-only baselines. [C] It performs abstractive-only summarization with GPT and early fuses features, offering no improvement over raw posts. [D] It encodes raw posts with a CNN only and discards behavioral inputs, achieving parity with behavioral-only models.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which combination of summarization method, fusion strategy, representation types, and outcome is correct for a user-level depression detector built from tweet histories? [A] Extractive-only summarization using TF-IDF, early fusion of LSTM text features with handcrafted behavior, and no gain over either single source. [B] Abstractive summarization via BART without extractive selection, late fusion of CNN-only text with RNN behavior, and worse performance than raw posts. [C] Hybrid extractive–abstractive summarization using BERT embeddings with k-means selection followed by BART generation, late fusion of CNN–BiGRU attention-based text representations with stacked BiGRU behavioral features, and better classification performance than using raw posts or behavioral signals alone. [D] No summarization, transformer text encoders with self-attention only, and improvement only over behavioral-only but not text-only baselines.

A user-level depression detection framework that condenses each user's tweet history via BERT embeddings with k-means selection followed by BART generation and late-fuses CNN–BiGRU attention-based text representations with stacked BiGRU behavioral features achieves better classification performance than using raw posts or behavioral signals alone.

A user-level depression detection framework that summarizes tweet histories with BERT embeddings and k-means followed by BART and that early-fuses CNN–BiGRU attention-based text representations with stacked BiGRU behavioral features does not outperform using raw posts or behavioral signals alone.

The user-level depression detection framework that condenses each user's tweet history via BERT embeddings with k-means selection followed by BART generation and _________(late/early) fuses CNN–BiGRU attention-based text representations with stacked BiGRU behavioral features yields better classification performance than using raw posts or behavioral signals alone.

For user-level depression detection, condensing tweet histories through hybrid extractive–abstractive summarization (BERT embeddings with k-means selection then BART generation) and late fusion of CNN–BiGRU attention-based text with stacked BiGRU behavioral features results in _________(better/worse) classification performance than using raw posts or behavioral signals alone.

Assertion (A): For user-level depression detection, condensing each user's tweet history via BERT embeddings with k-means selection followed by BART generation and late-fusing CNN–BiGRU attention-based text representations with stacked BiGRU behavioral features yields better classification performance than using raw posts or behavioral signals alone. input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement correctly describes how semantic representations of job titles are learned and what they achieve? [A] Representations are learned by a BERT-based encoder trained with distant supervision to predict co-occurring skills from vacancy texts, using token-level gating and negative sampling; they enable taxonomy-agnostic nearest-neighbor normalization without manual title labels and outperform generic sentence encoders on this task. [B] Representations are learned by an LSTM-based encoder trained with manual title labels, without token-level gating or negative sampling; they enable taxonomy-specific normalization via rule-based matching and do not outperform generic sentence encoders on this task. [C] Representations are learned by a BERT-based encoder trained to predict job titles from skills, using token-level gating but no negative sampling; they require manual title labels for normalization and show performance comparable to generic sentence encoders. [D] Representations are obtained by unsupervised averaging of word embeddings from vacancy texts; they support taxonomy-agnostic cluster-based normalization but only match the performance of generic sentence encoders on this task.

Choose the correct option (A–D): What configuration yields job-title representations that enable taxonomy-agnostic nearest-neighbor normalization without manual title labels and surpass generic sentence encoders on that task? [A] Unsupervised averaging of token embeddings from vacancy texts with cluster-based normalization and no negative sampling. [B] A BERT-based encoder trained to reconstruct titles from taxonomies using manual labels, with attention but no negative sampling. [D] A generic sentence encoder fine-tuned on unrelated corpora with taxonomy-specific nearest-centroid mapping.

Semantic representations of job titles learned by a BERT-based encoder trained with distant supervision to predict co-occurring skills from vacancy texts, using token-level gating and negative sampling, enable taxonomy-agnostic nearest-neighbor normalization without manual title labels and outperform generic sentence encoders on this task.

Semantic representations of job titles learned by a BERT-based encoder trained with distant supervision to predict co-occurring skills from vacancy texts, without token-level gating or negative sampling, require manual title labels for taxonomy-specific nearest-neighbor normalization and do not outperform generic sentence encoders on this task.

Assertion (A): Semantic representations of job titles learned by a BERT-based encoder trained with distant supervision to predict co-occurring skills from vacancy texts, using token-level gating and negative sampling, enable taxonomy-agnostic nearest-neighbor normalization without manual title labels and outperform generic sentence encoders on this task. Reason (R): Predicting co-occurring skills from vacancy texts under distant supervision aligns representations with job-title semantics, and token-level gating plus negative sampling increases discriminative power.

input: Assertion (A): Semantic representations of job titles learned by a BERT-based encoder trained with distant supervision to predict co-occurring skills from vacancy texts, using token-level gating and negative sampling, enable taxonomy-agnostic nearest-neighbor normalization without manual title labels and outperform generic sentence encoders on this task. Reason (R): Manual title labels and taxonomy-specific rules are required for training and normalization in this setting.

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): In evaluating face alignment performance over datasets, which statement correctly defines and characterizes AUC-α relative to mean error? [A] It is the area under the cumulative error distribution curve up to a specified threshold and provides a robust, outlier-insensitive single-value metric compared to mean error. [B] It is the area under the cumulative error distribution curve without any threshold and is more sensitive to outliers than mean error. [C] It is equal to the mean error and not an area-based metric. [D] It is not applicable to face alignment performance over datasets and requires multiple values to summarize results.

Choose the correct option (A–D): For face alignment evaluation over datasets, which description of AUC-α covers both its definition and its robustness compared to mean error? [A] A single-value, outlier-insensitive metric computed by integrating the cumulative error distribution only up to a specified threshold. [B] A single-value, outlier-prone measure identical to the mean error across samples. [C] A multi-valued summary obtained by integrating the entire cumulative error distribution without a threshold. [D] A single-value metric that ignores the cumulative error distribution and relies on full-range averaging.

For evaluating face alignment performance over datasets, AUC-α—defined as the area under the cumulative error distribution curve up to a specified threshold—is a robust, outlier-insensitive single-value metric compared to mean error.
When evaluating face alignment performance over datasets, AUC-α is a multi-valued metric derived from the full cumulative error distribution and is more outlier-sensitive than mean error.

The area under the cumulative error distribution curve up to a specified threshold (AUC-α) serves as a _________(robust, outlier-insensitive/fragile, outlier-sensitive) single-value metric for evaluating face alignment performance over datasets compared to mean error.

For evaluating face alignment performance over datasets, AUC-α is the area under the cumulative error distribution curve _________(up to a specified threshold/over the full error range) and provides a robust, outlier-insensitive single-value metric compared to mean error.

Assertion (A): AUC-α, defined as the area under the cumulative error distribution curve up to a specified threshold, is a robust, outlier-insensitive single-value metric for evaluating face alignment performance over datasets compared to mean error. Reason (R): Truncating the integration at a fixed error threshold reduces the influence of large-error samples that can disproportionately affect mean error.
output: A is True, R is True, and R explains A

input: Assertion (A): AUC-α, defined as the area under the cumulative error distribution curve up to a specified threshold, provides a robust, outlier-insensitive single-value metric for evaluating face alignment performance over datasets compared to mean error. Reason (R): It averages all errors equally across the entire range without any thresholding.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement best characterizes how face alignment accuracy depends on the initial face bounding box, including the relative impact of center versus scale perturbations and differences across methods? [A] Accuracy is highly sensitive to the initial box; small center perturbations generally degrade performance more than comparable scale changes; and robustness varies substantially across methods. [B] Accuracy is largely insensitive to the initial box; scale perturbations degrade performance more than center shifts; and robustness is uniform across methods. [C] Accuracy is highly sensitive to the initial box; scale perturbations generally cause larger degradation than center shifts; but robustness does not vary across methods. [D] Accuracy is insensitive to the initial box; center and scale perturbations have equal impact; and robustness varies only trivially across methods.

Choose the correct option (A–D): For face alignment, how do small perturbations of the initial face bounding box affect accuracy across methods? [A] Small center offsets typically reduce accuracy more than comparable scale changes, indicating high sensitivity to initialization and large differences in robustness across methods. [B] Small center offsets typically reduce accuracy less than comparable scale changes, indicating low sensitivity and little difference in robustness across methods. [C] Small scale changes typically reduce accuracy more than comparable center offsets, but robustness across methods is uniform, showing low sensitivity. [D] Small center and scale changes have negligible and similar effects, and robustness differences across methods are minimal.

Face alignment accuracy is highly sensitive to the initial face bounding box, with small center perturbations generally causing larger degradation than comparable scale changes, and robustness varies substantially across methods.

Face alignment accuracy is largely insensitive to the initial face bounding box, with small center perturbations generally causing less degradation than comparable scale changes, and robustness is uniform across methods.

Face alignment accuracy is highly sensitive to the initial face bounding box, with small center perturbations generally causing larger degradation than comparable scale changes and with _________(substantial variability in robustness across methods/uniform robustness across methods).
output: substantial variability in robustness across methods

input: Face alignment accuracy is highly sensitive to the initial face bounding box, with small center perturbations generally causing _________(larger degradation/smaller degradation) than comparable scale changes and with substantial variability in robustness across methods.

Assertion (A): Face alignment accuracy is highly sensitive to the initial face bounding box, with small center perturbations generally causing larger degradation than comparable scale changes and with substantial variability in robustness across methods. Reason (R): Small center perturbations are typically more damaging than scale changes, and robustness differs substantially across methods, which underlies the observed sensitivity.
output: A is True, R is True, and R explains A

input: Assertion (A): Face alignment accuracy is highly sensitive to the initial face bounding box, with small center perturbations generally causing larger degradation than comparable scale changes and with substantial variability in robustness across methods. Reason (R): Small scale changes are typically more damaging than center perturbations, and robustness is largely uniform across methods.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): What best describes the effect of training–test detector mismatch in face alignment and its implications when bounding boxes highly overlap? [A] It significantly degrades alignment performance even with high overlap, making detector consistency essential for fair evaluation and practical deployment. [B] It has negligible impact when overlap is high, so detector consistency is optional. [C] It improves performance through detector diversity, and consistency is mainly for reporting. [D] It matters only when overlap is low; with high overlap results are unchanged.

Choose the correct option (A–D): In face alignment, what does a mismatch between the training detector and the test-time detector imply when their bounding boxes have high overlap? [A] It has no effect if overlap is high, so consistency is only a reporting detail. [B] It can significantly degrade performance, indicating that detector consistency is crucial for fair evaluation and practical deployment. [C] It slightly improves robustness and promotes fairer evaluation. [D] It affects deployment but not evaluation when overlap is high.

Using different face detectors for training and testing significantly degrades alignment performance even when their bounding boxes highly overlap, making detector consistency essential for fair evaluation and practical deployment.

If bounding boxes from training and test detectors have high overlap, detector mismatch does not affect alignment performance, so consistency is unnecessary for fair evaluation and practical deployment.

output: even when bounding boxes have high overlap

input: Assertion (A): Detector consistency is essential for fair evaluation and practical deployment in face alignment. Reason (R): Mismatch between the training detector and the test-time detector significantly degrades alignment performance even when bounding boxes have high overlap.

input: Assertion (A): Mismatching the face detector between training and testing does not impair alignment performance when bounding boxes have high overlap, so detector consistency is not required for fair evaluation or deployment. input: Choose the correct option (A–D): In cascaded holistic face alignment, which statement correctly describes the effects of test-time initialization and cascade depth on localization accuracy and computational cost? [A] Including a mean-shape initialization and aggregating multiple initializations improves localization accuracy, and deeper cascades yield non-decreasing performance gains but higher computational cost. [B] Excluding a mean-shape initialization and using a single initialization improves localization accuracy, and deeper cascades yield decreasing performance gains with lower computational cost. [C] Including a mean-shape initialization and aggregating multiple initializations degrades localization accuracy, and deeper cascades produce unpredictable performance without affecting computational cost. [D] Using only random initializations improves localization accuracy, and cascade depth has no impact on performance or computational cost.

Choose the correct option (A–D): Which combined claim about cascaded holistic face alignment is accurate regarding test-time initialization strategy and cascade depth? [A] Aggregating multiple initializations without the mean shape improves localization accuracy, and increasing cascade depth can reduce performance while saving computation. [B] Including a mean-shape initialization and aggregating multiple initializations improves localization accuracy, and increasing cascade depth does not reduce performance but increases computational cost. [C] Using a single random initialization is sufficient for improved localization accuracy, and deeper cascades always degrade performance with neutral computational cost. [D] Mean-shape initialization should be avoided because it hurts localization accuracy, and additional cascade stages leave both performance and cost unchanged.

Assertion (A): In cascaded holistic face alignment, including a mean-shape initialization at test time and aggregating multiple initializations improves localization accuracy, and increasing cascade depth yields non-decreasing performance gains at the expense of computational cost. Reason (R): Aggregating multiple initializations including the mean shape enhances robustness, and each added cascade stage refines the estimate without undoing prior improvements, though each stage increases computation.

input: Assertion (A): In cascaded holistic face alignment, including a mean-shape initialization at test time and aggregating multiple initializations improves localization accuracy, and increasing cascade depth yields non-decreasing performance gains at the expense of computational cost. Reason (R): Increasing cascade depth reduces computational cost.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): In evaluating face analysis methods, which statement accurately contrasts tight ground-truth face boxes with practical detector outputs that include recovered missing detections? [A] Tight ground-truth boxes tend to overestimate robustness, whereas practical detector outputs (with recovered misses) reveal method sensitivities and meaningful performance differences. [B] Tight ground-truth boxes and practical detector outputs yield identical robustness estimates when missing detections are recovered. [C] Practical detector outputs inflate robustness compared with tight ground-truth boxes because they eliminate misses. [D] Tight ground-truth boxes reveal method sensitivities and performance differences, whereas practical detector outputs overestimate robustness.

Choose the correct option (A–D): To avoid overestimating robustness and to expose method sensitivities with meaningful performance differences in face evaluation, benchmarking should use which input? [A] Tight ground-truth face boxes without detector noise. [B] Ground-truth boxes expanded by a fixed margin. [C] Practical detector outputs that include recovered missing detections. [D] Synthetic boxes derived from saliency maps.

Evaluations that rely on _________(tight ground-truth face boxes/practical detector outputs) can overestimate robustness, whereas using practical detector outputs (including recovered missing detections) reveals method sensitivities and meaningful performance differences.

Benchmarking that includes recovered missing detections and uses _________(practical detector outputs/tight ground-truth face boxes) reveals method sensitivities and meaningful performance differences, while tight ground-truth boxes may overestimate robustness.

Assertion (A): Evaluating with tight ground-truth face boxes can overestimate robustness, whereas benchmarking with practical detector outputs that include recovered missing detections reveals method sensitivities and meaningful performance differences. Reason (R): Realistic detector outputs introduce localization noise and occasional misses, which expose vulnerabilities that perfect boxes conceal.
output: A is True, R is True, and R explains A

input: Assertion (A): Evaluating with tight ground-truth face boxes can overestimate robustness, whereas benchmarking with practical detector outputs that include recovered missing detections reveals method sensitivities and meaningful performance differences. Reason (R): Recovering missing detections makes practical detector outputs equivalent to ground-truth boxes, eliminating any difference in revealed sensitivities.
output: A is True, R is False

input: Choose the correct option (A–D): In small-sample, nonlinear retail settings, which approach yields more accurate daily demand forecasts for table grapes when trained with cross-validated hyperparameters on limited historical sales augmented by quantified weather, weekday/weekend, and price information? Support vector regression with radial basis function kernels, trained with cross-validated hyperparameters on limited historical sales augmented by quantified weather, weekday/weekend, and price information, yields more accurate daily demand forecasts for table grapes than artificial neural networks and decision trees in small-sample, nonlinear retail settings.
